title: Algebraic Integers of Bounded Height and Given Galois Group
speaker: Andy O’Desky
abstract: How many algebraic integers of bounded height have a minimal polynomial with a given Galois group? One approach to this problem is via Malle's conjecture. In this talk we will discuss an alternative approach using a construction with the Galois group's group algebra which has proven fruitful in recent years. We will explain how this construction lets one apply tools from harmonic analysis and the theory of toric varieties. Applications include a Malle-free determination of the asymptotic count for cyclic Galois groups. Time permitting, we will also explain how this construction gives a new perspective on cubic abelian polynomials.

proof_stages.label: Counting problem and known estimates
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines the counting function for algebraic integers of bounded height with prescribed transitive Galois group and surveys known fixed-degree asymptotics, cyclic prime-degree upper bounds, and the coefficient-height conjectural upper bound for proper subgroups of Sn.

proof_stages.label: Field-by-field heuristic for cyclic prime degree
proof_stages.summary: For cyclic groups of prime order, a Minkowski-height discriminant bound reduces bounded-height algebraic integers to fields of bounded discriminant, a lattice count inside each field gives an x^p factor divided by the square root discriminant, and Malle’s theorem for abelian groups suggests the leading exponent while leaving an unevaluable constant and poor generalization.

proof_stages.label: Main theorem and toric data
proof_stages.summary: The speaker introduces the algebraic unit group of the group algebra, its quotient by the finite group, Ono’s class-regulator-vector decomposition for torus adelic quotients, and toric/root height functions, then states the main asymptotics for C3 and for cyclic groups of order at least five with explicit constants to be defined later.

proof_stages.label: Normality rigidification of G-algebras
proof_stages.summary: Because G-fields do not form a base-change-stable moduli problem, the argument passes to G-algebras with a normal element, whose conjugates form a basis, and identifies their rational moduli with the homogeneous space of group-algebra units modulo G, where normality is generic and carries a simply transitive unit-group action.

proof_stages.label: Toric compactification and integral height zeta function
proof_stages.summary: The homogeneous space is compactified as a projective quotient with a natural divisor encoding algebraic integrality, and for abelian G this becomes a toric variety so that counting algebraic integers is recast as counting D-integral rational points of bounded toric height via a height zeta function.

proof_stages.label: Poisson summation and stable-flag residues
proof_stages.summary: Generalizing the Batyrev-Tschinkel method to integral points and quotient singularities, the proof applies multiplicative Poisson summation on the torus, separates the automorphic-character integral by Ono’s decomposition, analyzes the noncompact archimedean part through rational Fourier transforms with hyperplane poles, keeps only stable-flag iterated residues, and obtains the pole data and constants that Tauberian arguments convert into the asymptotic formula and error terms.

proof_stages.label: Exceptional C3 trace-normalized correspondence
proof_stages.summary: For the exceptional C3 case the speaker normalizes normal elements to trace one, draws the resulting two-dimensional hexagonal cocharacter lattice, identifies the quotient torus with the restriction torus for Q(sqrt(-3)), and derives an explicit bijection between trace-one cubic-algebra polynomials and quadratic polynomials together with reducibility, discriminant, and integrality correspondences.
